request.question: In between J Puttanna Kanagal and John Cassavetes, who acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby"?

title: Puttanna Kanagal Award
Kanagal, Mysore
Masanada Hoovu
Vijaya Bhaskar
Kappu Bilupu
P. H. Vishwanath
Puttanna Kanagal
Shivaram
Runamukthalu
John Cassavetes
sentences: The following is the list of Award winners for the Karnataka State Puttanna Kanagal award.
Kanagal was among the front runners in Kannada cinema's most successful film directors.
In his memory and honor, this award is presented to the directors every year during the Karnataka State Awards function.
Kanagal is a village in Mysore district, Karnataka.
It is in periyapatna taluk 30 km from Taluk place.
It is very close to Coorg and Hassan district.
It is located on the bank of the Cauvery River.
It is also birthplace of legendary Kannada film director Puttanna Kanagal, and his brother, lyricist Kanagal Prabhakara Shasthry.
Villagers are mainly farmers though many of the younger generation are working in other fields such as medical, engineering, teaching, police, army, and industries.
Masanada Hoovu (Kannada: ಮಸಣದ ಹೂವು ) ("Graveyard Flower") is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal, based on T. R. Subba Rao's novel of the same name.
It stars Jayanthi, Ambarish and Aparna in lead roles.
It was Puttanna Kanagal's last as a director.
His friend, director K. S. L. Swamy, completed the film.
"Masanada Hoove" won multiple awards at the 1985-86 Karnataka State Film Awards including Best Actress (Jayanthi) and Best Supporting Actor.
Vijaya Bhaskar (; 1924–2002) was an Indian music director and composer who composed music for several mainstream and experimental feature films in the Kannada film industry.
Scoring music for over 600 feature films, Bhaskar worked in Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, Marathi, Tulu and Konkani language films as well.
He was a part of all of popular Kannada film director KSL Swamy's (Ravi) movies and was also known for his association with acclaimed directors Puttanna Kanagal and Adoor Gopalakrishnan.
Kappu Bilupu (Kannada: ಕಪ್ಪು ಬಿಳುಪು, which means Black and White ) is a 1969 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal.
Based on a novel of the same name by Aryambha Pattabhi, this movie revolves around the contrasting behaviors of an identical, yet distinct pair of twins.
The film starred Kalpana in dual roles.
Puttanna himself remade the movie in Tamil as "Irulum Oliyum" and in Telugu as "Iddaru Ammayilu".
P. H. Vishwanath (Kannada: ಪಿ.
ಎಚ್.
ವಿಶ್ವನಾಥ್ ) is an Indian film director, writer and a producer who works in Kannada cinema.
Apart from films, Vishwanath has directed several documentaries and tele-series.
He started his film career as an assistant director to Puttanna Kanagal with "Dharmasere" (1979) being his first release.
Following this, he assisted for many films directed by Kanagal, Joe Simon, Hunsur Krishnamurthy and T. S. Nagabharana.

Puttanna Kanagal (born Shubraveshti Ramaswamiah Seetharama Sharma, 1 December 1933 – 5 June 1985), fondly called the 'Chitra Bramha' (God of Films, Kannada) was an Indian filmmaker and is considered as one of Kannada's cinema's greatest filmmakers.
S. Shivaram (born 1938), popularly known simply as Shivaram or Shivaramanna, is an Indian actor, producer and director whose Kannada cinema career has spanned six decades.
He has played roles including lead hero performances, character roles, comedic roles, as well as supporting parts.
He teamed up with his elder brother, S. Ramanathan and produced several films under the name Rashi Brothers apart from directing "Hrudaya Sangama" in 1972.
The brothers together produced few Bollywood films as well.
As an actor, Shivaram has the distinction of working with many stalwart directors out of which his association with Puttanna Kanagal is much remembered.
Runamukthalu (Kannada: ಋಣ ಮುಕ್ತಳು ) is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed and co-produced by Puttanna Kanagal starring Bharathi, Padmavasanthi and Ramakrishna.
The film is based on the novel "Runa" written by Anupama Niranjana.
The film was produced under the banner "Kanagal Creations".
John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.

Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.
He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).
He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.
His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.question: In between J Puttanna Kanagal and John Cassavetes, who acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby"?

hop1_docs: John Cassavetes | John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.  Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.  He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).  He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.  His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

Rosemary's Baby (film) | Rosemary's Baby is a 1968 American psychological horror film written and directed by Roman Polanski, based on the bestselling 1967 novel of the same name by Ira Levin.  The cast features Mia Farrow, John Cassavetes, Ruth Gordon, Sidney Blackmer, Maurice Evans, Ralph Bellamy, Angela Dorian, Clay Tanner, and, in his feature film debut, Charles Grodin.  The film chronicles the story of a pregnant woman who suspects that an evil cult wants to take her baby for use in their rituals.

Puttanna Kanagal Award | The following is the list of Award winners for the Karnataka State Puttanna Kanagal award.  Kanagal was among the front runners in Kannada cinema's most successful film directors.  In his memory and honor, this award is presented to the directors every year during the Karnataka State Awards function.

Apart from films, Vishwanath has directed several documentaries and tele-series.  He started his film career as an assistant director to Puttanna Kanagal with "Dharmasere" (1979) being his first release.  Following this, he assisted for many films directed by Kanagal, Joe Simon, Hunsur Krishnamurthy and T. S. Nagabharana.

It is located on the bank of the Cauvery River.  It is also birthplace of legendary Kannada film director Puttanna Kanagal, and his brother, lyricist Kanagal Prabhakara Shasthry.  Villagers are mainly farmers though many of the younger generation are working in other fields such as medical, engineering, teaching, police, army, and industries.

"Masanada Hoove" won multiple awards at the 1985-86 Karnataka State Film Awards including Best Actress (Jayanthi) and Best Supporting Actor.
response.hop2_query: Confirm that John Cassavetes acted in many Hollywood films and appeared as Guy Woodhouse in the film "Rosemary's Baby" (1968).
hop2_docs: John Cassavetes | John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.  Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.  He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).  He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.  His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

Rosemary's Baby (film) | Rosemary's Baby is a 1968 American psychological horror film written and directed by Roman Polanski, based on the bestselling 1967 novel of the same name by Ira Levin.  The cast features Mia Farrow, John Cassavetes, Ruth Gordon, Sidney Blackmer, Maurice Evans, Ralph Bellamy, Angela Dorian, Clay Tanner, and, in his feature film debut, Charles Grodin.  The film chronicles the story of a pregnant woman who suspects that an evil cult wants to take her baby for use in their rituals.

Proxy (film) | Proxy is a 2013 American horror film directed by Zack Parker.  The movie had its world premiere on September 10, 2013 at the Toronto International Film Festival.  It stars Alexia Rasmussen as a pregnant young woman who joins a support group after suffering a stillbirth due to a vicious attack.  The filmmakers describe Proxy as a spiritual successor to the horror film "Rosemary's Baby", and its main character Esther Woodhouse is named after the earlier film's protagonist Rosemary Woodhouse.

Look What's Happened to Rosemary's Baby | Look What's Happened to Rosemary's Baby (also known as Rosemary's Baby II) is a 1976 American made-for-television horror film and a sequel to Roman Polanski's 1968 film "Rosemary's Baby" starring Stephen McHattie, Patty Duke, George Maharis, Ruth Gordon and Ray Milland.  The film premiered as the "ABC Friday Night Movie" on October 29, 1976.

Faces (film) | Faces is a 1968 drama film, written and directed by John Cassavetes and starring John Marley, Cassavetes' wife Gena Rowlands, Fred Draper, Seymour Cassel and Lynn Carlin.  Both Cassel and Carlin received Academy Award nominations for this film.  Cassavetes was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay for "Faces".  The film was shot in high contrast 16 mm black and white film stock.  In 2011, it was added to the National Film Registry of the Library of Congress.

Z Channel: A Magnificent Obsession | Z Channel: A Magnificent Obsession is a 2004 documentary film about Los Angeles pay cable channel Z Channel which was directed by Xan Cassavetes, daughter of Hollywood director and actor John Cassavetes.  It was screened out of competition at the 2004 Cannes Film Festival.

Mia Farrow | María de Lourdes "Mia" Villiers Farrow (born February 9, 1945) is an American actress, activist and former fashion model.  She first gained notice for her role as Allison MacKenzie in the television soap opera "Peyton Place" and gained further recognition for her subsequent short-lived marriage to Frank Sinatra.  An early film role, as Rosemary in Roman Polanski's "Rosemary's Baby" (1968), saw her nominated for a BAFTA and a Golden Globe for Best Actress.  She went on to appear in films such as "John and Mary" (1969), "Follow Me! " (1972), "The Great Gatsby" (1974) and "Death on the Nile" (1978).
hop2_titles: Look What's Happened to Rosemary's Baby
